unit_name: Introduction to Systems and Phase Plane Analysis
hook: You've already mastered techniques for solving a single differential equation — but most real systems, from predator-prey ecosystems to coupled electrical circuits, involve multiple interacting quantities changing at once. A single equation simply can't capture that richness. This unit asks: what happens when everything depends on everything else, and how can we *see* the behavior of a system without solving it explicitly?
concept: The unifying idea is that a system of differential equations defines a vector field in the plane, and understanding that field — its equilibria, its flow, its stability — tells you the long-run story of any solution, often without finding a formula. This is captured formally by *phase plane analysis and qualitative theory of dynamical systems*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn how to translate multi-variable physical problems into a first-order system and interpret what each equation represents.
Master the elimination technique to reduce a linear system into a single higher-order ODE that you can solve with familiar tools.
You will learn to sketch solution curves directly in the $xy$-plane, revealing the global behavior of a system without computing explicit solutions.
Classify equilibrium points as stable, unstable, or saddle-like by analyzing eigenvalues, and predict whether nearby solutions converge or diverge.
Apply linearization to approximate a nonlinear system near an equilibrium and use the resulting linear model to determine local stability.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Limit Cycles and the Poincaré-Bendixson Theorem (H)
topic_roadmap.description: You will use the Poincaré-Bendixson Theorem to identify when trajectories must settle into a closed periodic orbit, revealing sustained oscillations in nonlinear systems.
